content.full_text:
j_
1.f
a11rornia
Fair Political
Practices Commission
May 26,
1992
Ronald R.
Ball
Acting City Attorney
City of Carlsbad
1200 Carlsbad Village Drive
Carlsbad,
CA
92008—1989
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
A—92—291
Dear Mr.
Ball:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice on
behalf of Carlsbad Mayor Pro Tern Ann J.
Kulchin concerning her
duties under the conflict—of-interest provisions of the Political
Reform Act
(the “Act”) •1
Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place.
In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us.
The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Ogelsby
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
71.)
QUESTION
Does Mayor Pro Tern Kulchin have an economic interest in the
Kelly Family Trust,
where her adult child’s mother-in-law manages
the trust?
CONCLUS ION
According to your facts,
Mayor Pro Tern Kuichin does not have
an economic interest in the Kelly Family Trust.
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
428 J Street, Suite 800
• P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660


Our File No.
A-92-291
Page
2
FACTS
Mayor Pro Tern Kulchin’s adult daughter, Leslie, married and
became part of the Sippel family.
Lucia Sippel, Leslie’s mother-
in-law,
manages the Kelly Family Trust (the “trust”),
a large
landowner in the jurisdiction.
You stated that the mayor pro tern
has not received income from either her daughter or the trust in
the past
12 months.
You stated the mayor pro tern’s daughter is
financially independent and does not rely on the mayor pro tern as
a source of income.
Finally,
you confirmed on May 20,
1992 that
the mayor pro tern was not a beneficiary of the trust.
You have asked whether the Kelly Family Trust is an economic
interest of the mayor pro tern.
You stated that the trust owns
land that may be developed and that the trust may also be asked to
dedicate some property to the city for a park.
Consequently,
you
anticipate that the trust will be appearing before the city
council on matters in the near future.
ANALYSIS
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making,
or attempting to use their official
position to influence a governmental decision in which they have a
financial interest.
Section 87103 defines a “financial interest”
as:
An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family
or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts and
other than loans by a conunercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more ira value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official


Our File No. A-92-29l
Page
3
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
Cd)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
(e)
Any donor of,
or any intermediary or agent
for a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within
12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.
For purposes of this section, indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a
public official,
or by a business entity or trust
in which the official,
the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
indirectly,
or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.
Section 87103.
According to your facts, the issue in question will not
involve any investment or property interests of the mayor pro tern.
(Section 87103(a),
(b)
and (d).)
Moreover,
you stated that the
mayor pro tern has not received income or gifts from the trust or
income from her daughter.
2
Consequently, the mayor pro tern will
not have a conflict of interest in the decisions concerning the
Kelly Family Trust by virtue the economic interests set forth in
Section 87103(a),
(b)
and (d).
The Mayor Pro Tem’s Daughter
You have asked whether the interests of the mayor pro tern’s
adult child create a conflict of interest for the mayor pro tern.
While financial interests may be imputed to an elected official
under Section 87103,
such a result will only occur with respect to
the interests of the official’s immediate family.
2
The definition of “gift” does not include gifts from an
official’s spouse, child, parent, grandparent, grandchild,
brother,
,
parent—in—law, brother—in—law,
sister—in—law,
nephew, niece, aunt, uncle, or first cousin or the spouse of any
such person; provided that a gift from any such person shall be
considered a gift if the donor is acting as an agent or
intermediary for any person not covered by this paragraph.
(Section 82028(b) (3).)


Our File No.
A-92-291
Page
4
“Immediate family” is defined in Section 82029 as the
official’s spouse and dependent children.
A child is considered a
“dependent child” for purposes of the Act if the child is under 18
and the elected official is entitled to claim the dependent child
as such for income tax purposes.
(Tremlett Advice Letter,
No.
1-89-386.)
Thus,
the financial interests of the mayor pro tern’s
adult child will not be disqualifying with respect to the
governmental decisions of the mayor pro tern.
(See also,
Smith
Advice Letter,
No.
1—89—541.)
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—5901.
Sincerely,
SH:JWW: aa
Scott Hallabrin
ting General Counsel
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10c per page.
John
W. Wallace
Counsel,
Legal Division
